attributes.stem: A 28-year-old woman presents to her General Practice clinic with ongoing symptoms of anxiety despite treatment. She was diagnosed with generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) six months ago and has been titrated up to and taking sertraline 100 mg daily for the past four months. She reports minimal improvement in her symptoms and continues to experience excessive worry about various aspects of her life, restlessness, and difficulty concentrating. She also reports persistent nausea since starting sertraline, which has affected her appetite. She has no significant medical history and takes no other regular medications. On examination, she appears mildly anxious but is otherwise well with vital signs within normal limits.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in pharmacological management?
attributes.explanation:
This patient has been diagnosed with generalized anxiety disorder (GAD) and has had a minimal response to first-line treatment with an SSRI (sertraline). Additionally, she is experiencing significant side effects (nausea affecting her appetite). The most appropriate next step in pharmacological management is to switch to venlafaxine, a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI). Venlafaxine is considered an effective second-line treatment for GAD when SSRIs are ineffective or not tolerated. The presence of side effects with minimal therapeutic benefit makes switching to a different class of medication more appropriate than dose escalation.

Increasing sertraline to 200 mg daily could theoretically provide additional benefit, as the maximum dose for GAD is 200 mg. However, this would likely worsen the patient's already problematic nausea and gastrointestinal side effects. Given the minimal response after four months at 100 mg and the presence of troublesome side effects, dose escalation is not the best approach.

Adding buspirone is not typically recommended as a second-line treatment for GAD. While it can be effective for some patients and has a favorable side effect profile, there is less evidence supporting its use compared to SNRIs like venlafaxine. It would also not address the current side effects from sertraline.

Starting pregabalin is a potential option for GAD, but it is generally considered a third-line treatment after SSRIs and SNRIs have been tried. It may be considered if venlafaxine is also ineffective or poorly tolerated.

Commencing diazepam or other benzodiazepines is not recommended for long-term management of GAD due to the risk of dependence, tolerance, and other side effects. Benzodiazepines should only be used for short-term relief of severe anxiety symptoms while waiting for first-line treatments to take effect.

It's important to note that alongside pharmacological management, cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) should be considered as part of the treatment plan for GAD. CBT has been shown to be effective in managing anxiety symptoms and can provide patients with long-term coping strategies.
learningPoints: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs) are the first-line pharmacological treatment for generalized anxiety disorder (GAD).
Venlafaxine, a serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (SNRI), is an effective second-line treatment for GAD when SSRIs are ineffective or not tolerated.
Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) should be considered alongside pharmacological treatments for GAD.
references: Andrews G, Bell C, Boyce P, Gale C, Lampe L, Marwat O, Rapee R, Wilkins G. Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Psychiatrists clinical practice guidelines for the treatment of panic disorder, social anxiety disorder and generalised anxiety disorder. Australian & New Zealand Journal of Psychiatry. 2018 Dec;52(12):1109-72.
